narrative:
**HEADLINE**
Overall macro risk remains elevated at 6.2, with three stories at High level—Japan carry trade, critical minerals weaponization, and AI power grid constraints—presenting the largest tail risks.

**STORIES WITH LEVEL CHANGES**
No intraday level changes recorded.

**ELEVATED OR HIGHER RISKS TO NOTE**
Treasury basis trades (hidden leverage), private credit opacity, developing-world debt stress, China's deflationary spillover, AI circular financing, hidden consumer debt, food/fertilizer fragility, ASML lithography concentration, and sea lane/subsea cable exposure all remain at Elevated (level 6). France/eurozone fragility and CRE maturity walls sit at level 5 (Elevated). Water as an underpriced input flagged at level 5.

**TOP WATCH**
Japan fiscal/JGB/carry trade at level 7 (High) remains the most systemically consequential story given yen carry unwind potential and global funding shock risk.

stories.name: Treasury basis trade (hidden leverage)

indicators.label: SOFR minus EFFR (%)
MOVE index (Treasury volatility)
SEC central clearing on track (0 ok / 1 slipping / 2 delayed)

stories.summary: Hedge funds run a highly leveraged arbitrage between Treasury cash bonds and futures. It is a major source of liquidity, but a spike in funding costs or Treasury volatility can force rapid unwinds, as briefly happened in March 2020 and again in 2025.

stories.name: Japan fiscal / JGB / carry trade

indicators.label: USD/JPY (yen strengthening = lower = risk)
30-year JGB yield (%)
Takaichi plan financing clarity (0 ok / 1 unclear / 2 disorderly)

stories.summary: Decades of near-zero rates made Japan the funding leg of a vast global carry trade. As the BoJ normalizes and JGB yields rise, fiscal financing gets harder and carry unwinds can transmit stress worldwide - the August 2024 mini-crash was a preview.

stories.name: Private credit's opaque expansion

indicators.label: US high-yield OAS (%, ≈310bps)
Direct-lending default rate (%)
Large-fund redemption gates (0 none / 1 isolated / 2 widespread)

stories.summary: Lending has migrated from banks to lightly regulated private-credit funds, where assets are marked infrequently and leverage is opaque. Defaults are rising from a low base; the risk is that losses surface suddenly rather than being visible in real time.

stories.name: Developing-world debt crisis

indicators.label: Broad US dollar index (FRED DTWEXBGS)
US 10y real yield (TIPS, %)
New IMF programs / missed coupons (0 calm / 1 rising / 2 cluster)

stories.summary: A strong dollar, high real rates, and heavy foreign-currency debt loads leave many developing economies vulnerable to a sudden stop. A cluster of sovereign defaults or new IMF programs would signal the slow-burn crisis turning acute.

stories.name: China's price spillover (deflation or cost-push)

indicators.label: China PPI YoY (%) - large swing either way is worse
USD/CNY (yuan depreciation = higher = risk)
New tariff/anti-dumping actions (0 calm / 1 rising / 2 escalating)

stories.summary: China's factory-gate prices spill into global goods markets in BOTH directions: years of PPI deflation exported disinflation and squeezed competitors, while a sharp 2026 reversal to positive PPI (energy/commodity cost-push) now exports input-cost pressure. Either large swing is destabilising; the indicator is scored on the size of the move, not its sign.

stories.name: Critical minerals as a weapon

indicators.label: Rare-earth export license flow (0 flowing / 1 slowing / 2 frozen)
Nov-2025 pause status (0 extended / 1 expiring / 2 lapsed)
US domestic RE producer blacklist (0 none / 1 entity-specific ban / 2 sector-wide ban)
Ex-China heavy rare-earth price premium (x China)

stories.summary: China dominates the mining and especially the processing of rare earths and critical minerals, and has shown willingness to use export licensing as leverage. Supply chains for magnets and defense inputs have little near-term substitute.

stories.name: Stablecoins & Treasury demand

indicators.label: Largest stablecoin max peg deviation (%)
Total USD stablecoin supply ($bn)
GENIUS implementation friction (0 smooth / 1 gaps / 2 problems)

stories.summary: Dollar stablecoins have become sizeable holders of short-term Treasuries, linking crypto plumbing to the funding market. A large redemption wave could force rapid T-bill selling; the GENIUS Act's implementation is the key regulatory variable.

stories.name: AI build-out circular financing

indicators.label: US investment-grade OAS (%) - big-issuer proxy
AI capex-vs-revenue gap concern (0 closing / 1 wide / 2 widening fast)
GPU-backed debt refinancing stress (0 none / 1 signs / 2 acute)

stories.summary: AI infrastructure spending vastly exceeds current AI revenue, increasingly financed with debt and circular vendor arrangements. If monetization disappoints or GPU-backed financing strains, the capex cycle could reverse sharply.

stories.name: CRE maturity wall / regional banks

indicators.label: CMBS office delinquency rate (%)
Regional-bank CRE stress (0 calm / 1 isolated / 2 cluster)

stories.summary: A wall of commercial real-estate loans - especially office - matures into higher rates and lower valuations, concentrated at regional banks. Rising delinquencies and 'extend and pretend' practices defer, rather than resolve, the losses.

stories.name: France / eurozone fragility

indicators.label: OAT-Bund 10y spread (%)
Govt stability (0 stable / 1 fragile / 2 collapse-or-snap-election)
Rating action (0 stable / 1 negative outlook / 2 downgrade)

stories.summary: France combines a wide deficit, a fragmented parliament, and recurring budget standoffs, widening its spread over Germany. Political instability or a rating downgrade would test eurozone cohesion at its second-largest economy.

stories.name: Hidden consumer debt

indicators.label: Subprime auto 60+ day delinquency (%)
US unemployment rate (%)
BNPL delinquency trend (0 stable / 1 rising / 2 spiking)

stories.summary: Beneath headline figures, subprime auto and buy-now-pay-later delinquencies are climbing, and the labor market is the swing factor. A rise in unemployment would convert stretched-but-current borrowers into defaults quickly.

stories.name: Gold / de-dollarization

indicators.label: Gold price (USD/oz)
USD share of FX reserves (%, IMF COFER)
Reserve-weaponization / settlement shift (0 none / 1 talk / 2 action)

stories.summary: Central banks are accumulating gold and building non-dollar settlement arrangements at the margin. It is a slow erosion rather than a sudden shift, but reserve 'weaponization' events can accelerate diversification away from the dollar.

stories.name: Insurance retreat

indicators.label: Insurer non-renewals / exits (0 stable / 1 rising / 2 accelerating)
US single-family mortgage delinquency (%, FRED DRSFRMACBS)
Catastrophe-loss pressure (0 normal / 1 elevated / 2 severe season)

stories.summary: Insurers are retreating from climate-exposed markets through non-renewals and exits, shifting risk onto homeowners and state backstops. Severe catastrophe seasons strain the system and feed into housing and mortgage stress.

stories.name: Pensions / demographics

indicators.label: Social Security / pension reform shock (0 none / 1 debate / 2 cuts)
Pension-fund leverage event, UK-2022 type (0 none / 1 stress / 2 blowup)
Large public-pension funded ratio (%)

stories.summary: Aging demographics and underfunded public pensions create slow fiscal pressure, while leveraged liability-driven strategies can blow up fast - as the UK gilt/LDI crisis showed in 2022. Benefit-reform fights are the political pressure valve.

stories.name: AI power & the grid bottleneck

indicators.label: Grid interconnection wait (months)
Data-center moratoria / backlash (0 none / 1 spreading / 2 widespread)
AI power-demand pressure (0 easing / 1 tight / 2 acute)

stories.summary: Data-center electricity demand is colliding with multi-year grid interconnection queues and slow capacity additions. Whether this is an acute shortfall or eases depends on the pace of AI build-out versus new supply - forecasts diverge widely.

stories.name: Copper & the electrification deficit

indicators.label: Copper price (USD/lb, COMEX HG=F)
Major mine disruptions (0 none / 1 some / 2 cluster)
Deficit timing (0 surplus / 1 balanced / 2 deficit)

stories.summary: Electrification and AI infrastructure point to structural copper deficits as demand outruns new mine supply, which takes a decade to build. Price is the live signal; mine disruptions and inventory draws would confirm tightening.

stories.name: Water - the underpriced input

indicators.label: Drought in a chip/farm hub (0 none / 1 emerging / 2 acute)
Key reservoir / basin stress (0 normal / 1 low / 2 critical)
Transboundary water dispute escalation (0 calm / 1 tense / 2 crisis)

stories.summary: Water is systematically underpriced yet essential to chip fabrication, agriculture, and power. Droughts in key hubs (Taiwan's fabs, major farming regions) and transboundary disputes can disrupt supply chains in ways markets rarely price in advance.

stories.name: Food & fertilizer fragility

indicators.label: Food-price trend (0 falling / 1 elevated / 2 spiking)
China fertilizer export curbs (0 lifted / 1 in place / 2 tightened)
Hormuz/Gulf disruption to fertilizer inputs (0 clear / 1 disrupted / 2 cut)

stories.summary: Food security depends on concentrated fertilizer supply chains - potash, phosphate, and gas-derived nitrogen - exposed to export curbs and shipping chokepoints. Curbs or input shocks feed through to prices with a lag.

stories.name: The lithography (ASML) chokepoint

indicators.label: Taiwan / cross-strait tension (0 calm / 1 elevated / 2 crisis)
Export-control escalation (0 stable / 1 tightening / 2 retaliation)
Single-point concentration (0 easing / 1 high / 2 extreme)

stories.summary: ASML is the sole maker of EUV lithography machines, with Zeiss the sole optics supplier - a single point of failure for advanced chips. Export controls, retaliation, or any disruption to this chain would ripple across the entire tech economy.

stories.name: Sea lanes & undersea cables

indicators.label: Hormuz/Red Sea status (0 open / 1 contested / 2 closed)
WTI crude oil (USD/bbl, CL=F)
Undersea cable incidents (0 none / 1 threatened / 2 cut)

stories.summary: A few maritime chokepoints and undersea cables carry most global trade and data. The 2026 Hormuz/Red Sea disruptions showed how quickly closures spike energy and shipping costs; cable cuts are a parallel, under-watched vulnerability.

stories.name: Clearinghouses (financial chokepoint)

indicators.label: Equity volatility (VIX)
Treasury volatility (MOVE)
Treasury-clearing rollout stress (0 smooth / 1 friction / 2 problems)

stories.summary: Central counterparties now concentrate the system's risk by design, and Treasury central-clearing mandates will route more activity through them. In a volatility spike, procyclical margin calls can amplify stress - they are a systemic single point of failure.

items.label: Direct-lending default rate (%)
items.rationale: The rubric calls for the latest direct-lending default rate as a percent; the Proskauer Index is the most granular, loan-level US direct-lending series available and reports 2.73% for the most recent complete quarter (Q1 2026). Dissenting readings from Fitch (6.0%, April 2026) and With Intelligence (~5% 'true' rate) are materially higher because they include distressed exchanges and liability-management exercises that Proskauer counts separately, so 2.73% is the most conservative loan-count-based anchor.
items.fact: Proskauer Private Credit Default Index: 2.73% for Q1 2026 (Jan 1 – Mar 31 2026), tracking 697 senior-secured/unitranche US loans totalling $189.2bn in original principal; up from 2.46% in Q4 2025 and 1.84% in Q3 2025. Source: https://www.proskauer.com/report/proskauers-private-credit-default-index-reveals-rate-of-273-for-q1-2026, published April 27 2026.

dissent.view: Fitch Ratings reported US private credit default rate hit a record 6.0% in April 2026, with private-credit-backed corporates at 9.2% for full-year 2025 — methodology includes distressed exchanges.
With Intelligence estimates 'true' default rate approaches 5% once selective defaults and liability-management exercises are included, vs. headline below 2% (2026 outlook).
Moody's Analytics estimates 2025 private credit default rate in the range of ~1.6%–4.7% depending on treatment of distressed restructurings (April 28 2026).
Morgan Stanley warned direct lending defaults could surge to 8% driven by AI disruption of software sector (March 16 2026).

items.label: GENIUS implementation friction (0 smooth / 1 gaps / 2 problems)
items.rationale: All primary agency rules are still in proposed/comment stage within 15 days of the July 18, 2026 statutory finalization deadline, with no final rules published by any agency and the Federal Reserve having issued no prudential-framework NPRM at all — a documented gap among the primary regulators. This maps squarely to rubric level 1 (GAPS: rules still in proposed/comment stage near the deadline, with documented inter-agency divergence in readiness).
items.fact: As of July 3, 2026 — 15 days before the statutory July 18, 2026 deadline — all major agency rulemakings remain at the NPRM (proposed rule) stage, not final rules. OCC published its NPRM on March 2, 2026 (comment period closed May 1); FDIC approved its NPRM on April 7, 2026 (comments closed June 9); FinCEN/OFAC issued a joint NPRM in April 2026 (comments closed June 9); Treasury issued its state-comparability NPRM in April 2026. Critically, the Federal Reserve had not published any substantive proposed rule for applications or prudential frameworks as of the last confirmed tracker update (KPMG, May 4, 2026: 'No proposals have yet been released by the FRB regarding applications or prudential frameworks under the GENIUS Act'). Six agencies must finalize simultaneously with comment periods closing only weeks before the deadline (vaasblock.com, ~June 24, 2026; angelinvestorsnetwork.com, ~June 19, 2026). Sources: occ.gov bulletin-2026-3; fdic.gov FIL April 7 2026; federalregister.gov 2026-06974; treasury.gov SB0435; kpmg.com GENIUS Act alert May 2026.

dissent.view: Chapman & Cutler tracker characterizes all comment periods as closed by June 9, 2026, implying agencies are in active final-rule drafting and could theoretically meet the deadline; some sources treat the deadline as achievable if agencies act in the final weeks.

items.label: Large-fund redemption gates (0 none / 1 isolated / 2 widespread)
items.rationale: The rubric threshold for level 2 is >=3 of the largest funds gating in the same quarter, OR combined gated AUM >$100B. At least 5 of the largest non-traded BDCs (BCRED, ASIF, Apollo Debt Solutions, HPS, North Haven) enforced 5% quarterly repurchase caps simultaneously in Q2 2026 with combined AUM well exceeding $100B, satisfying both prongs of the WIDESPREAD criterion.
items.fact: In Q1-Q2 2026, at least 6 large non-traded BDCs/private credit funds actively capped repurchases at or below 5% quarterly: BCRED (~$82B AUM, 10% demand prorated at 5% in Q2 per SEC SC TO-I/A filed June 4 2026), Ares Strategic Income Fund (~$22.6B, 14.4% demand prorated at 5% in Q2 per Altswire June 2026), Apollo Debt Solutions BDC (~$25B, 16.8% demand prorated at 5% in Q2 per Altswire June 2026), HPS Corporate Lending Fund (~$26B, 9.3% demand capped Q1), North Haven Private Income Fund (11.6% demand capped Q2 per PitchBook/Altswire June 2026), and Blue Owl OBDC II ($1.6B, permanently closed). CRS (Congress.gov, updated April 2026) states 'multiple redemption restrictions by private credit funds in early 2026 have generated widespread concerns and congressional attention.' Moody's also cut BDC sector outlook to Negative in April 2026.

dissent.view: Some analysts (CAIA/iCapital April 2026, PGIM April 2026, Fitch Feb 2026) argue gates are functioning as designed -- a structural feature, not a crisis signal -- noting BDC leverage ratios and asset coverage remain healthy (avg D/E 0.71x, coverage cushion 38.6%), distinguishing a 'wrapper problem' from systemic distress and arguing this should NOT be read as a funding-market breakdown corroborated by rate/spread signals.

items.label: Takaichi plan financing clarity (0 ok / 1 unclear / 2 disorderly)
items.rationale: A materially widening tail (0.2 pt vs 0.05 pt prior) signals a soft/tailing auction but does not yet meet the rubric's 'bid-to-cover <2 or long tail' threshold for a clear failed auction; yields are elevated on fiscal concerns but the 4.5 bp single-session move and the 12-month cumulative ~130 bp rise, combined with unresolved funding-path clarity on the full stimulus envelope, squarely maps to rubric level 1 (funding path unspecified / yield up materially on fiscal news, auctions still clearing but with stress signals).
items.fact: The July 2, 2026 10-year JGB auction produced a 'weak' result: the auction tail widened sharply to 0.2 point from 0.05 point at the prior June auction, and the benchmark 10-year yield rose 4.5 bps to 2.745% (highest since May 22), with investor concern explicitly attributed to the government's 'big spending plan' (Business Recorder / Reuters, 2026-07-03). The 10-year yield has risen ~130 bps over 12 months. Separately, Takaichi's May 2026 ~3 trillion yen supplementary budget promised no net increase in bond issuance, but the funding path for the broader stimulus plan remains contested. No emergency BOJ operation was reported.

dissent.view: May 25, 2026 CNBC report: Takaichi's 3-trillion-yen supplementary budget explicitly promised 'no extra borrowing overall', suggesting funding IS specified — which would argue toward a 0 rating for that specific budget element.

items.label: Treasury-clearing rollout stress (0 smooth / 1 friction / 2 problems)
items.rationale: No deadline extension has been announced and the SEC has reaffirmed the Dec 2026 / Jun 2027 dates, ruling out a score of 2; however, TBAC's May 2026 report identifies an active documentation and bilateral-agreement backlog as the largest implementation challenge, SIFMA's exemptive relief request is pending SEC decision, and the industry survey flagged unresolved inter-affiliate and CCA-rulebook clarity issues as potential blockers—squarely matching the rubric's 'documentation backlog, pending exemptive relief' criteria for a score of 1.
items.fact: TBAC Charge Q2 2026 (May 2026, Treasury.gov): 'Largest challenge from an implementation standpoint continues to be the amount of legal work and documentation associated with the bilateral agreements between participants'; SEC published pending exemptive relief requests from SIFMA and IIB on April 17, 2026 with comment deadline May 29, 2026; deadlines remain Dec 31 2026 (cash) and Jun 30 2027 (repo). SIFMA/DTCC/BNY/Broadridge ValueExchange pulse survey (Aug 2025, n=330): net positive US domestic familiarity (71% 'very familiar'), but survey flagged that 'if these and other issues aren't resolved by early 2026, firms' ability to build and be ready on time may be impacted,' with operational/technology workloads described as the last to complete. SEC Chair Atkins confirmed deadlines intact as of March 10, 2026.

dissent.view: SEC Chair Atkins (March 2026) and SEC.gov implementation page characterise progress as on-track with no suggestion of impending failure, which could support a score of 0 if documentation friction resolves quickly

items.label: BNPL delinquency trend (0 stable / 1 rising / 2 spiking)
items.rationale: The 29 bps YoY rise in Affirm 30+ DPD confirms delinquency is rising, satisfying the rubric's Level 1 condition. The rise does not clear the >100 bps YoY threshold for Level 2, and cohort charge-offs (~3.5%) are contained below the 5% spike threshold, so Level 2 is not warranted.
items.fact: Affirm FQ3'26 Shareholder Letter (SEC 8-K, filed ~May 2026): '30+ day delinquencies excluding Peloton and Pay in X loans increased 29 basis points year over year and increased 7 basis points quarter over quarter to 2.8%.' Allowance for credit losses was 6.0% of loans held for investment; recent cohort cumulative net charge-offs tracking ~3.5% of cohort GMV — well below the 5% charge-off spike threshold.

dissent.view: LendingTree/Benzinga (March 2026 survey): 47% of BNPL users reported a late payment in the past year, up from 41% in 2025 — suggesting broader consumer stress than Affirm's 30+ DPD series captures; however, this is a self-reported survey metric, not a formal delinquency rate, and does not override hard loan-level data.

Richmond Fed (Feb 2026, EB 26-05): 'There is indicative evidence from consumer surveys that late payment and delinquency behavior among BNPL users has increased since 2023,' but concludes no clear evidence of elevated stress through 2025 in broader consumer credit markets.

items.label: CMBS office delinquency rate (%)
items.rationale: The rubric requires the latest CMBS office delinquency rate as a percent; 11.20% is the most recently quoted office-sector figure from Trepp/multihousingnews (Feb 2026), down from Jan's record 12.34% driven by large office loan modifications/extensions. Confidence is medium because the May 2026 report excerpt does not explicitly state the current office sub-rate, leaving a 3-month gap in office-specific data.
items.fact: Per multihousingnews.com citing Trepp data (updated ~June 2026 page): 'Office posted the largest decline, falling 114 basis points to 11.20 percent, pulling back from January's all-time high of 12.34 percent' — February 2026 office CMBS delinquency rate = 11.20%; January 2026 all-time high = 12.34%. No more recent explicit office sub-rate figure was recoverable from available excerpts.

dissent.view: January 2026 CMBS office delinquency rate was 12.34% — the all-time high per Trepp, per multihousingnews.com

items.label: Catastrophe-loss pressure (0 normal / 1 elevated / 2 severe season)
items.rationale: YTD insured losses of ~$20bn are below — not above — the 10-year Q1 average, and no single event breached the $10bn threshold, satisfying the NORMAL (0) criteria on both the aggregate and single-event tests. The 2026 Atlantic hurricane season (peak Aug-Oct) has not yet produced any major events as of the assessment date.
items.fact: Global insured catastrophe losses totaled $20 billion in Q1 2026, 26% below the 10-year decadal average of $26 billion, with no single event exceeding $10 billion (Gallagher Re, Q1 2026 Natural Catastrophe and Climate Report, April 2026). Aon independently confirmed at least $20 billion for Q1 2026 (Aon Global Catastrophe Recap Q1 2026, April 2026).

dissent.view: Aon describes Q1 2026 insured losses as 6% ABOVE the 21st-century average (vs. Gallagher Re's 26% below 10-year average); the divergence reflects different baseline periods, not different dollar totals — both cite ~$20bn.

items.label: GPU-backed debt refinancing stress (0 none / 1 signs / 2 acute)
items.rationale: Two rubric-1 triggers are confirmed: banks offloading AI/data-center debt at a discount (FT, May 2026) and CoreWeave as a named borrower with a documented $4.2B 2026 refinancing wall on GPU-backed paper. No confirmed default, distressed exchange, or failed refinancing (rubric-2) has been reported as of July 3 2026.
items.fact: Banks including JPMorgan, Morgan Stanley, and SMBC were actively trying to offload AI data-center debt at a discount via SRT structures and direct loan sales as exposure approached internal limits (FT, reported via resultsense.com, May 6 2026). CoreWeave, the largest GPU-backed borrower, carried a $4.2B refinancing wall due in 2026 on pre-IPO GPU-collateralized facilities with underlying H100 collateral having depreciated 50-70% (davefriedman.substack.com, April 19 2026; levelheadedinvesting.com). CoreWeave's 5yr CDS peaked at 881bps in December 2025 before falling to ~452bps (Bloomberg/Yahoo Finance, ~June 2026). The $4.2B wall was partially addressed via the March 31 2026 DDTL 4.0 ($8.5B, rated A3 by Moody's) but no confirmed default or failed refinancing has been reported.

dissent.view: CoreWeave's DDTL 4.0 closed at investment-grade (A3/Moody's, March 31 2026), CDS tightened sharply, and Applied Digital bonds priced at 7% vs prior 10%, suggesting conditions are normalizing rather than stressed.

items.label: Insurer non-renewals / exits (0 stable / 1 rising / 2 accelerating)
items.rationale: The state count of ~13 active non-renewal markets falls squarely within the 5–15 range (RISING = 1); no top-10 insurer has fully exited a major state in 2026 (State Farm remains in CA under a rate settlement), and AM Best's Stable outlook plus falling reinsurance rates rule out the ACCELERATING = 2 threshold.
items.fact: A June 2026 industry aggregation (climatecosmos.com) identifies 13 US states with active non-renewal waves in 2026 — CA, FL, TX, LA, NC, GA, OK, NE, MN, CO, and others — corroborated by state-specific disclosures: CA DOI moratorium expired Jan 7 2026 enabling new non-renewals; TX and LA enacted transparency laws effective Jan 1 2026 in response to ongoing non-renewals; NC saw Nationwide drop 10K policies; CO enacted wildfire-model disclosure law July 2026. NAIC issued a nationwide data call (deadline June 15 2026) covering cancellations and non-renewals across all states. AM Best revised US homeowners outlook from Negative to Stable in December 2025 (AM Best Market Segment Report, accessed Jan 2026 per NAIC filing commentary).

dissent.view: Some markets show partial stabilization: Florida Citizens hit a record-low 385K policies and received an 8.7% rate decrease approval, and reinsurance rates fell 10-25% at June 1 2026 renewals, suggesting the non-renewal wave may be peaking rather than spreading, supporting a STABLE = 0 reading in select markets.

items.label: Regional-bank CRE stress (0 calm / 1 isolated / 2 cluster)
items.rationale: Two FDIC-confirmed community/regional bank failures in the trailing 6 months places this in the ISOLATED band (1-2 firm-specific events); however, CRE as the explicit primary driver is not confirmed by supervisors in either case, and no systemic CRE warning has been issued, precluding CLUSTER (2). The rubric scores ISOLATED (1) given at least one failure (Community Bank & Trust) involved capital/governance stress consistent with CRE concentration pressures even if not explicitly labeled as such by the FDIC. [cross-check (asi1): two reads disagreed (1 vs 0); held for review]
items.fact: FDIC failed-bank list (last updated May 1, 2026) records exactly 2 U.S. bank failures in 2026: Metropolitan Capital Bank & Trust (Chicago, IL, closed Jan 30 2026, ~$261M assets) and Community Bank & Trust - West Georgia (LaGrange, GA, closed May 1 2026, ~$288M assets). Community Bank & Trust received a Fed enforcement action on governance/capital prior to closure; neither FDIC nor Fed press releases explicitly cite CRE concentration as the primary failure driver. No supervisory declaration of systemic regional-bank CRE stress has been issued. Source: https://www.fdic.gov/bank-failures/failed-bank-list (updated May 1, 2026); Banking Dive / American Banker May 4 2026.

dissent.view: Industry analysts (VaaSBlock, BankHealthData, CFO Brew Feb 2026) characterize regional bank CRE stress as ongoing and concentrated at smaller institutions, with latent distress understated by a factor of four per Wharton/OFR research -- suggesting underlying CRE pressure is higher than formal failure count implies, though no named firm-specific CRE failure has been confirmed by primary supervisory sources.

items.label: Subprime auto 60+ day delinquency (%)
items.rationale: The February 2026 Fitch figure of 6.80% is the latest hard monthly datapoint available from the primary ABS-tracking source; it sits near a 32-year record high, confirming severe and sustained stress concentrated in the subprime ABS segment.
items.fact: Fitch Ratings subprime auto ABS 60+ day delinquency rate was 6.80% in February 2026, down from a record 6.90% in January 2026, per Wolf Street (May 19, 2026) citing Fitch. March 2026 data had not yet been released as of that report.

dissent.view: S&P Global Ratings reported full-year 2025 average subprime auto ABS 60+ day delinquency at 6.18%, describing it as 'at an all-time high' — a lower figure because it is a full-year average rather than a peak-month reading.
Auto Finance News reported December 2025 subprime 60+ day delinquency at 6.74% (Fitch), slightly below the Jan-Feb 2026 readings but itself a then-record.

items.label: Large public-pension funded ratio (%)
items.rationale: The Milliman 100 PPFI is the preferred primary-source index for the 100 largest U.S. public pension plans; the April 30, 2026 reading of 87.6% is the most current published figure and maps directly to the rubric's request for a representative large-plan funded ratio expressed as a percent. Equable Institute's broader-universe estimate of 82.5% (end-2025) is consistent in order of magnitude but covers a wider, lower-funded universe and is less current.
items.fact: Milliman 100 Public Pension Funding Index (PPFI): funded ratio of the 100 largest U.S. public pension plans rebounded to 87.6% as of April 30, 2026, up from 83.7% at end of March 2026, driven by estimated aggregate investment returns of 4.7% in April. Source: Milliman PPFI release via Business Wire, published June 3, 2026 (https://lasvegassun.com/news/2026/jun/03/milliman-analysis-public-pension-funded-ratio-rebo/).

dissent.view: Equable Institute estimates the aggregate funded ratio for all U.S. state and local retirement systems improved from 78.0% in 2024 to 82.5% for fiscal year 2025 (as of December 31, 2025), covering a broader universe of 245+ plans including weaker-funded smaller systems.

items.label: New IMF programs / missed coupons (0 calm / 1 rising / 2 cluster)
items.rationale: One new IMF facility (Burkina Faso RSF, Feb 2026) was approved in the window alongside augmentation of its existing ECF, and no sovereign missed-coupon event was confirmed, placing the count at 1-2 distress-adjacent actions. This maps to RISING (value=1) under the rubric: 1-2 new IMF arrangements with no confirmed missed coupon.
items.fact: In the trailing 6 months (Jan–Jul 2026), the IMF approved a new RSF arrangement for Burkina Faso on Feb 18 2026 (SDR 90.3 mn, alongside a pre-existing ECF augmented in May 2026 by 50% of quota); Sri Lanka completed combined 5th+6th EFF reviews; Argentina completed its 2nd EFF review (EFF approved Apr 2025). No confirmed sovereign missed coupon or selective-default rating action in this window was found. IMF DSA list as of Mar 31 2026 shows 9 countries 'in debt distress' (pre-existing stock). Sources: imf.org PR 26/054 (Mar 2026), imf.org PR 26/170 (May 2026), imf.org PR 26/165 (May 2026), imf.org DSA list Mar 31 2026.

dissent.view: The Burkina Faso RSF could be classified as a supplemental climate facility rather than a sovereign-distress program, which would push the distress-event count toward 0 (CALM).

items.label: Rating action (0 stable / 1 negative outlook / 2 downgrade)
items.rationale: Two of the three major agencies (Fitch on 2025-09-12 and S&P on 2025-10-17) executed outright rating cuts on France within the trailing 12 months, satisfying rubric level 2 (actual rating cut in trailing 12 months). Moody's independently placed France on negative outlook (level 1 condition also met), but the executed downgrades by Fitch and S&P dominate and map unambiguously to the highest band.
items.fact: Fitch downgraded France from AA- to A+ on 12 Sep 2025 (stable outlook); S&P downgraded France from AA- to A+ on 17-18 Oct 2025 (stable outlook, confirmed May 2026); Moody's affirmed Aa3 but revised outlook to negative on 24 Oct 2025. Sources: fitchratings.com (Sep 2025), spglobal.com article 'France Ratings Lowered To A+/A-1' (Oct 2025), ratings.moodys.com/ratings-news/453186 (Oct 2025), Bloomberg 2026-05-30.

dissent.view: TradingEconomics shows S&P at A+ with 'stable' outlook as of mid-2026, which some may read as post-downgrade stabilisation rather than an ongoing negative signal — but the downgrade itself occurred within the trailing 12 months regardless of current outlook label.

items.label: Reserve-weaponization / settlement shift (0 none / 1 talk / 2 action)
items.rationale: The Russian freeze predates 2026 and outright seizure has not occurred; no new-target sovereign reserve action is evidenced. BRICS Pay operational pilot launch and the Unit token pilot constitute new non-dollar settlement system launches — squarely Band 1 (TALK/new settlement launches) not Band 2 (new seizure or major economy operationally off USD).
items.fact: No new sovereign reserve seizure executed in 2026; Russian ~$280-300B reserves remain frozen (since 2022) but not confiscated (Brookings, June 2025; CFR, Nov 2025). BRICS Pay began partial operational deployment in 2026 integrating CIPS/UPI/Pix/SPFS, with full launch targeted for New Delhi summit Sept 2026; the gold-backed 'Unit' token was piloted Oct 31 2025 (bricspayment.ru; riotimesonline.com, July 2026).

dissent.view: Some sources (informedclearly.com, watcher.guru) claim BRICS Pay has 'already reduced USD usage in intra-bloc trade by roughly two-thirds' and frame it as fully operational — overstating actual deployment vs. pilot/partial status confirmed by bricspayment.ru and GIS Reports.

items.label: Social Security / pension reform shock (0 none / 1 debate / 2 cuts)
items.rationale: The trustees report worsened the depletion date (moved one quarter earlier to Q4 2032) and reform is actively debated (H.R. 9187, Senate proposals), but no cuts have been enacted or automatically triggered, placing this squarely at rubric level 1 (DEBATE). Level 2 requires an executed automatic reduction or enacted legislation cutting benefits, neither of which has occurred.
items.fact: The 2026 Social Security Trustees Report (released June 9, 2026, ssa.gov/oact/trsum/) projects the OASI Trust Fund will be depleted in Q4 2032 — one quarter earlier than the 2025 report — at which point only 78% of scheduled benefits would be payable. No automatic benefit reduction has been triggered and no legislation enacting cuts has been enacted; the Bipartisan Social Security Commission Act of 2026 (H.R. 9187) has been introduced but not passed.

dissent.view: CBO (Feb 2026) projects OASI depletion in FY2032 with an immediate 28% cut upon exhaustion — slightly more severe than the trustees' 22% figure — suggesting the fiscal gap may be understated by the trustees
CRFB projects a 24% across-the-board cut at depletion vs. trustees' 22%, reflecting different modeling assumptions

items.label: Ex-China heavy rare-earth price premium (x China)
items.rationale: The rubric asks for the ex-China HREE price premium as a multiple of the China price. The most-cited authoritative figures from Argus (via Reuters) and Benchmark Minerals bracket the mid-2026 multiple at 4-5x for dysprosium, with IEA and industry analysts extending the range to 6x in European markets; 4.5 is the defensible central estimate for the current period.
items.fact: Argus data cited by Reuters (Oregon Group, ~June 2026): ex-China dysprosium oxide ~US$1,450/kg vs China domestic ~US$119-123/kg (CREIA, June 29 2026), a ratio of ~11x on spot; but Argus/Reuters characterises the overall HREE ex-China premium as 'x4 to x5' for dysprosium and terbium since April 2025 controls. Benchmark Minerals (March 24 2026) independently pegs the 2025 dysprosium CIF North America at 4.4x EXW China, forecasting 8.3x by 2027.

dissent.view: CREIA domestic price (June 29 2026) of ~US$120/kg Dy vs Argus ex-China ~US$1,450/kg implies ~12x on illiquid spot offers - likely reflects thin market and distressed procurement rather than a clearing price multiple
IEA reports European prices 'up to six times' Chinese prices, and InvestingNews analysts cite '4-6x'; the upper end of 6x is supported by European bilateral contract data
Benchmark Minerals forecasts premium doubling to 8.3x by 2027 from 4.4x in 2025, implying mid-2026 is transitionally above 4.4x but well below 8x

items.label: Export-control escalation (0 stable / 1 tightening / 2 retaliation)
items.rationale: Both the US/allied side (MATCH Act, BIS Blackwell guidance, VEU revocations) and the Chinese side (June 11 tech-transfer retaliation law, June 22 US-firm entity-list additions, mineral enforcement tightening) have enacted or formally proposed new measures in the trailing 3 months; China's June 2026 actions constitute documented statutory countermeasures explicitly framed as retaliation against Western controls, satisfying rubric level 2. The ASML China revenue volume drop from 36% to 19% in one quarter corroborates material disruption rather than mere headline noise.
items.fact: Within the trailing 3 months (Apr–Jul 2026): US side — MATCH Act introduced Apr 2 2026 to ban DUV tools to China (CNBC/Reuters, Apr 7 2026); BIS June 1 2026 guidance tightening Blackwell chip controls on China-HQ firms globally (Al Jazeera, Jun 1 2026). China side — State Council enacted tech transfer controls Jun 11 2026 with explicit statutory retaliation authority (MOFCOM/FT via abhs.in, Jun 11 2026); MOFCOM added 10 US firms to export control entity list Jun 22 2026, described by experts as retaliation (Al Jazeera, Jun 22 2026); MOFCOM Announcement No. 26 (Jun 24 2026) tightened strategic mineral export enforcement, effective Jul 1 2026 (geopolitechs.org); 50% domestic equipment procurement mandate for Chinese chipmakers threatening ~$18B in US equipment sales (thenextweb.com, May 2026). ASML China share fell from 36% (Q4 2025) to 19% (Q1 2026), corroborating volume signal.

dissent.view: East Asia Forum (Mar 2026) argues the White House has 'cooled down' chip controls in 2026, prioritising trade talks over new BIS rules, suggesting the executive branch posture is more TIGHTENING (level 1) than full RETALIATION — China's countermeasures are framed as defensive/symbolic rather than a hard equipment ban on ASML specifically.

items.label: Hormuz/Red Sea status (0 open / 1 contested / 2 closed)
items.rationale: Hormuz transit is severely below normal but recovering under US naval oversight with active peace talks underway — fitting CONTESTED (1) on both the physical and diplomatic dimensions. Brent at ~$71, near pre-war lows and falling, is explicitly inconsistent with a closure scenario and caps the rating at 1 per rubric.
items.fact: IMF PortWatch recorded 27 vessel transits on June 28 (32% of ~84/day normal); by July 1 Windward logged 43 transits in one day; a US official confirmed crude flows exceeded 10 million bpd under US military oversight; Brent crude fell to ~$71/bbl on July 2-3, its lowest since pre-war Feb 27, as indirect US-Iran talks progressed in Doha. (Sources: IMF PortWatch June 28; Windward Daily Intelligence July 1 2026; TradingEconomics/Reuters July 2-3 2026)

dissent.view: straits.live labels the strait 'effectively closed' as of July 3, citing June 28 AIS data at 32% of normal and 508 vessels anchored; characterises pressure index at 84 (extreme)

items.label: Nov-2025 pause status (0 extended / 1 expiring / 2 lapsed)
items.rationale: The truce's nominal expiry (Nov 10 2026) is >90 days away, ruling out the time-based trigger for Score 1; however, China's addition of key US rare earth firms to its export control list constitutes a partial breach — precisely the entity-list escalation the rubric cites as a Score 1 indicator. Score 2 is unwarranted because the broad suspension of Oct 2025 controls has not been formally rescinded.
items.fact: China placed MP Materials, USA Rare Earth, and 8 other US entities on its export control list and barred 46 US firms from government procurement in retaliation for Pentagon designations — described by SCMP (~June 19 2026) as 'one of the most significant escalations since the Busan truce' — while the nominal Nov 10 2026 suspension of the Oct 2025 rare-earth controls remains formally in effect (White House Fact Sheet, Nov 5 2025; MOFCOM Announcement, Nov 7 2025). Source: https://www.scmp.com/news/china/diplomacy/article/3357974/us-china-rare-earth-clash-20-fragile-truce-tested-tit-tat-moves-return

dissent.view: CSIS (May 2026) and TechTimes (May 2026) characterise the Nov 2025 truce as still formally in force, with the Oct 2025 suspension holding and only the pre-existing April 2025 controls (never suspended) causing residual disruption — no breach language used.
Anderseninstitute (Apr 2026) notes Beijing redirected dual-use controls toward Japan in early 2026 but does not characterise the US-facing truce as breached.

items.label: Rare-earth export license flow (0 flowing / 1 slowing / 2 frozen)
items.rationale: Licenses are being issued but only to a small whitelist of large producers covering specific approved customer lists, with all other applicants facing non-automatic case-by-case review far exceeding 30 days — a textbook rubric-1 condition. No evidence of categorical halt (rubric 2), and no evidence of normal timelines for most applicants (rubric 0). [cross-check (asi1): two reads disagreed (1 vs 0); held for review]
items.fact: China's MOFCOM April 2025 Announcement No. 18 controls on 7 HREEs and derivative magnets remain fully in force; the October 2025 expansions are suspended until Nov 10 2026 (MOFCOM Ann. No. 70). As of Dec 2025, only three producers (JL Mag, Ningbo Yunsheng, Zhong Ke San Huan) received 'general licences' covering specific client lists; all other exporters remain on case-by-case review with opaque timelines exceeding 45-60 days. Jan 2026 MOFCOM Announcement No. 1 [2026] added new controls on samarium/gadolinium/lutetium compounds. Merics (Apr 2026) confirms approvals remain 'slow and targeted'. Sources: Reuters/Mining.com Dec 2 2025; MOFCOM Ann. 70 Nov 7 2025; Merics Apr 2026; Andersen Institute Apr 29 2026.

dissent.view: Duramag (Dec 2025) characterises HREE magnet licensing as near-frozen for heavy-REE products like SmCo and Dy/Tb-bearing NdFeB, with approvals 'rare, documentation-intensive, and unpredictable' and timelines often exceeding 3 months — suggesting a reading closer to rubric 2 for HREE-specific magnets

items.label: Single-point concentration (0 easing / 1 high / 2 extreme)
items.rationale: Zero operational <=3nm capacity exists outside Taiwan as of mid-2026; Arizona's only running fab is 4nm and overseas 3nm fabs are 12-18 months from production. This places outside-Taiwan share of leading-edge (<=3nm) capacity well below 10% (effectively ~0%), satisfying the EXTREME rubric condition of '<10% outside Taiwan AND no operational overseas leading-edge fab.'
items.fact: As of Q1-Q2 2026, all five of TSMC's volume-producing 2nm fabs are in Taiwan (Hsinchu x2, Kaohsiung x3); TSMC Arizona Fab 21 produces 4nm only; Arizona Fab 2 (3nm) is equipment-installation phase with commercial production targeted H2 2027; Kumamoto Japan Fab 2 (3nm) targets 2028. SemiAnalysis (Jan 2026) states 'over 90% of TSMC total capacity is in Taiwan... over 90% of global advanced-node capacity.' TSMC 2025 Annual Report (investor.tsmc.com, 2026) confirms overseas fabs are Nanjing (mature), Arizona (4nm only), JASM Kumamoto (22/28/12/16nm). No operational <=3nm fab exists outside Taiwan. Source: TSMC 2025 Annual Report (investor.tsmc.com); TSMC Q1 2026 6-K (SEC, Apr 2026); Focus Taiwan Apr 16 2026; SemiAnalysis Jan 2026.

dissent.view: TSMC CEO C.C. Wei projected (July 2025) that the completed Arizona gigafab cluster will account for ~30% of 2nm-and-more-advanced capacity — but this is a long-run build-out target (2029+), not current capacity, and does not contradict the current 0% reading.

items.label: Undersea cable incidents (0 none / 1 threatened / 2 cut)
items.rationale: The rubric requires >=1 major cable currently severed and unrepaired for level 2; no such confirmed active cut is found in sources as of July 2026. Multiple credible threat signals (shadow fleet activity, Hormuz shipping congestion, Iran threats, AUKUS protection pact, paused cable projects) satisfy rubric level 1 (THREATENED).
items.fact: No confirmed major submarine cable currently severed and unrepaired as of July 2026. The most recent confirmed multi-cable cut event was Red Sea on Sept 6 2025 (SEA-ME-WE-4, IMEWE, FALCON GCX, EIG near Jeddah), since rerouted. 2026 sources (TeleGeography May 2026, AGBI Mar 2026, CNN May 2026, Forbes Jun 2026) document elevated threat at Hormuz and Red Sea -- including Meta pausing 2Africa Pearls Gulf work and Iranian sovereign-toll threats -- but no named cable confirmed severed and unrepaired. Australian Defence Minister stated cables are 'being cut at an unprecedented rate' (CNN, May 31 2026) without specifying a current unrepaired cut.

dissent.view: Australian Deputy PM Marles (May 31 2026) said cables are being cut 'at an unprecedented rate' over 18 months, which some outlets frame as an ongoing crisis rather than a threat posture -- but no specific currently-severed named cable is cited.

items.label: AI power-demand pressure (0 easing / 1 tight / 2 acute)
items.rationale: The DOE Section 202(c) emergency order on 18 May 2026 authorising forced curtailment of data-centre loads in the PJM footprint is a confirmed, grid-operator-level curtailment action — the precise trigger for band 2 under the rubric. Seattle's power-scarcity-driven moratorium on new data-centre connections is a corroborating confirmed physical-policy impact, not merely a forecast.
items.fact: On May 18 2026, the U.S. DOE issued a Federal Power Act Section 202(c) emergency order authorising PJM Interconnection to curtail data centres and other large loads with backup generation during a grid-stress event driven by hot weather plus 40+ GW of planned plant outages (Utility Dive, 19 May 2026). Separately, Seattle City Council unanimously passed an emergency moratorium on new data-centre siting (>20 MVA) on 9 June 2026 citing grid-capacity and utility-rate impacts (Seattle City Council, 9 June 2026). The IEA 'Electricity 2026' report (late-June 2026) independently documents >2,500 GW of projects stalled in global grid queues and notes that 'many power systems are already experiencing rising congestion-related curtailment' (iea.org/reports/electricity-2026). LBNL 'Queued Up 2026 Edition' records ~8,200 U.S. projects (1,312 GW) in queue as of end-2025, with average wait times exceeding five years (emp.lbl.gov).

dissent.view: SemiAnalysis (June 2026) argues that projects with signed interconnection agreements and site control continue to progress on schedule, and that moratoriums land overwhelmingly in markets with negligible real capacity; they characterise broad 'crisis' framing as exaggerated. The curtailment event was a one-off emergency window, not sustained forced curtailment.

items.label: China fertilizer export curbs (0 lifted / 1 in place / 2 tightened)
items.rationale: The mid-March 2026 expansion of bans to NK blends and additional phosphate categories is a NEW broadened restriction added within the trailing 3 months, satisfying rubric level 2; the May urea quota is a heavily gated, price-floored allocation -- not a lifting -- while the NDRC phosphate suspension continues through August 2026, confirming tightening remains in effect.
items.fact: NDRC convened a special meeting on Dec 11 2025 suspending phosphate fertilizer exports through August 2026 (S&P Global/Platts, Dec 11 2025; SunSirs/China Chemical News). In mid-March 2026 Beijing extended restrictions to nitrogen-potassium blends and additional phosphate varieties (Reuters/Bloomberg via Taipei Times Mar 21 2026; Exiger Apr 16 2026). In late May 2026 a tightly managed urea quota of ~1.5-1.6M tonnes for June-August was issued with a mandatory $660/t FOB price floor -- a controlled reopening, not a lift. Phosphate suspension to August 2026 remains in force. Between half and three-quarters of China fertilizer exports are now restricted (Exiger/Reuters customs data analysis).

dissent.view: Philippines reported China indicated fertilizer exports would not be restricted, though Chinese authorities never formally confirmed this position
Some read the late-May urea quota issuance as a 'liberalization' or return to export, potentially arguing for rubric level 1 rather than 2

items.label: Data-center moratoria / backlash (0 none / 1 spreading / 2 widespread)
items.rationale: Rubric level 2 requires a statewide moratorium enacted OR >10 jurisdictions with enacted pauses/cancellations. No statewide moratorium is currently in force, but 100+ local communities have enacted local moratoriums — far exceeding the 10-jurisdiction threshold for the second prong of level 2. Jurisdictions with enacted local pauses include municipalities across NY (e.g. Lysander/Onondaga County), VA, GA, TX, PA, NJ, MI, OH, and many others per the cited sources.
items.fact: As of July 3 2026, no statewide moratorium is enacted and in force (Maine's LD 307 was vetoed by Gov. Mills on April 24 2026; New York's S10642 passed both chambers June 4 2026 but remains unsigned by Gov. Hochul). However, Sanders/AOC's March 25 2026 press release (datacenterdynamics.com) states 'more than 100 local communities around the country have enacted moratoriums on data centers,' corroborated by Multistate (July 2026) reporting 'dozens of municipalities have moved ahead with local construction pauses' and the Rockefeller Institute (June 2026) confirming 14 states have considered statewide moratoriums. Primary source: datacenterdynamics.com, March 25 2026; rockinst.org, June 2026; multistate.us, July 2026.

dissent.view: As of April 2026, Maine was described as 'the only state to pass a statewide data center moratorium into law' — but this predates the April 24 veto and is now superseded; no statewide moratorium is in force as of July 3 2026.

Encino Environmental (April 2026) states '12 states have taken up data center moratoriums and 11 have been unsuccessful,' suggesting most state-level bills have failed; only local-level pauses are broadly enacted.

items.label: Deficit timing (0 surplus / 1 balanced / 2 deficit)
items.rationale: A forecast surplus of +96,000 mt exceeds the +100kt threshold marginally - it sits just inside the BALANCED band, but ICSG characterises it as a surplus and the April 2026 figure is the authoritative current reading; mapped to rubric band 0 (SURPLUS). Note: at 96kt this is within ~4kt of the +/-100kt balanced boundary, but ICSG language is unambiguously 'surplus' so band 0 is the correct call.
items.fact: ICSG Copper Market Forecast 2026-2027 (April 2026 press release, icsg.org): global refined copper surplus of ~96,000 metric tons in 2026, reversing its prior October 2025 deficit forecast of -150,000 mt; attributed to lower-than-anticipated copper usage (demand growth revised down to 1.6% from 2.1%) and higher secondary refined production.

dissent.view: ICSG October 2025 forecast: refined copper deficit of -150,000 mt for 2026 - superseded by April 2026 update
J.P. Morgan projects a steeper deficit of -330,000 mt for 2026 driven by AI data-centre demand
Goldman Sachs base case: 2026 surplus of ~160,000 mt

items.label: Drought in a chip/farm hub (0 none / 1 emerging / 2 acute)
items.rationale: Taiwan's WRA rationing order at Hsinchu — the world's leading chip-fab hub — is a confirmed water-rationing order at a fab region, satisfying the rubric's level-2 threshold directly. The US Southwest concurrently registers D3 (extreme) drought on the USDM, also independently meeting level 2.
items.fact: Taiwan's Water Resources Agency imposed formal water rationing affecting chipmaking hub Hsinchu County after the driest winter in 75 years; Baoshan Reservoirs inflow at only 77% of 2021 century-drought levels; emergency diversion measures active through July 2026 and agricultural irrigation postponed in southern Taiwan (Taipei Times, 2026-03-27; Taiwan News, 2026-06-02). Simultaneously, the US Drought Monitor (2026-06-30) shows extreme drought D3 expanding across the Four Corners states (NM, UT, CO, AZ) with record-low April snowpack across 8 western states (droughtmonitor.unl.edu; drought.gov).

dissent.view: Taiwan government said it aimed to prevent 'zoned domestic rationing before end of June' and that emergency pipeline transfers had 'bought time' — implying no full production curtailment confirmed at fabs themselves as of late March 2026
US Southwest monsoon forecast leans above-average, suggesting short-term drought may improve in July 2026, potentially moderating the D3 reading

items.label: Grid interconnection wait (months)
items.rationale: LBNL reports the median IR-to-COD duration crossed the 5-year (60-month) threshold for 2025 COD projects, up from 'over 4 years' in the prior edition; 60 is the minimum integer consistent with 'over 5 years' since the full-precision figure is not broken out in the public summary.
items.fact: LBNL Queued Up: 2026 Edition (data through end of 2025), emp.lbl.gov/queues: 'For the regions with available data, the median duration from IR to COD was over 5 years for projects built in 2025.' Over 5 years = >60 months.

dissent.view: LBNL 2025 Edition (data through 2024) reported median IR-to-COD of 'over 4 years' (~48-55 months) for 2018-2024 COD projects; Loopback Systems commentary on same report cites 55 months average for 2024 COD projects.
Environment+Energy Leader (July 2026) states 'national median wait time for interconnection studies alone exceeded 40 months in 2024' -- a studies-only metric, narrower than full IR-to-COD.

items.label: Hormuz/Gulf disruption to fertilizer inputs (0 clear / 1 disrupted / 2 cut)
items.rationale: The strait is physically contested and fertilizer/sulfur/ammonia flows are severely reduced (vessels at 32% of normal, ammonia under special scrutiny), satisfying rubric band 1 disruption. Band 2 is capped out by the rubric itself because a signed MOU exists and Brent at ~$72 is NOT elevated at crisis levels, so the oil price signal does not corroborate a full closure rating.
items.fact: Per IMF PortWatch (via straits.live, updated 03 Jul 2026 1222Z), 27 vessels transited the Strait of Hormuz on June 28 — 32% of the pre-crisis ~84/day norm; war-risk insurance at 8.0x pre-crisis; ~820 ships stranded; June 17 Islamabad MOU signed but failed to hold durably; Brent crude at ~$72/bbl, near pre-war levels ($71.32 on Feb 27). Fertilizer/ammonia ships explicitly deprioritized behind oil/LNG in any partial reopening (Aawsat/Kpler, ~June 18 2026).

dissent.view: straits.live labels the strait 'effectively closed' (day 124), which could argue for band 2 on physical grounds alone

hormuzstraitmonitor.com crisis timeline notes VP Vance claimed on ~June 21 that 'the straits really are open' after a record 16-20 million barrels transited in a single day, suggesting a brief band-0 window that did not sustain

items.label: Key reservoir / basin stress (0 normal / 1 low / 2 critical)
items.rationale: At 29% of capacity, Lake Mead is at or below the rubric's <30% CRITICAL threshold; the reservoir is also operating below the ~1,050 ft Tier 2 shortage trigger and declining, with confirmed hydropower capacity reductions already in effect at Hoover Dam.
items.fact: USBR Lower Colorado Region July 2026 daily operations table (usbr.gov/lc/region/g4000/hourly/levels.html) shows Lake Mead at Hoover Dam elevation of 1,044.49 feet on July 1, 2026, with storage of ~7,290 thousand acre-feet — confirmed at 29% of full-pool capacity by the USBR Weekly Hydrologic Update dated June 29, 2026 (1,047.14 ft, 29%).

dissent.view: uswaterlevels.com reports Lake Mead at 47.44 ft elevation and 3.9% capacity as of May 20, 2026 — this appears to be a data error (possibly a USGS gauge reading in a different datum or a site bug) and directly contradicts all USBR primary data showing ~1,044-1,047 ft elevation.

items.label: Major mine disruptions (0 none / 1 some / 2 cluster)
items.rationale: Two top-20 mines — Grasberg and Kamoa-Kakula — have confirmed, physically measured production cuts active right now (sub-65% and sub-55% of normal rates respectively), and multiple institutions have formally forecast a 2026 market deficit as a direct consequence; this satisfies the rubric's CLUSTER threshold of >=3 mines OR lost output large enough that institutions forecast a market deficit.
items.fact: Two major top-20 copper mines have confirmed, ongoing production cuts as of early July 2026: (1) Grasberg (world's 2nd-largest copper mine, Indonesia/Freeport-McMoRan) — Grasberg Block Cave still in phased ramp-up after Sep 2025 mudslide; Freeport's May 2026 earnings update (mining.com, 2026-05-08) cut 2026 copper output target to ~700 million lb from 1 billion lb, aiming only 65% capacity in H2 2026 and full restart delayed to early 2028; (2) Kamoa-Kakula (DRC/Ivanhoe Mines, world's 3rd-largest) — seismic flooding from May 2025 forced guidance cut to 290,000–330,000 t in 2026 vs. original ~600,000 t target, with Q1 2026 output at 71,417 t (annualising to ~286,000 t), per Ivanhoe Q1 2026 release (ivanhoemines.com, 2026-04-13) and Metalnomist (2026-05-30). Multiple institutions (Benchmark Mineral Intelligence, Goldman Sachs, Citi) forecast a copper market deficit of 400,000+ t in 2026, corroborated by price near record highs. TFM/CMOC (DRC) strike (June 1–5, 2026) resolved quickly with minimal production impact and is NOT counted as an active outage.

dissent.view: Freeport's Jan 2026 guidance had projected ~1.0 billion lb for full-year 2026, implying a less severe cut than the May 2026 revision to ~700 million lb; some analysts (Goldman Sachs late-2025) initially modelled only ~270,000 t lost at Grasberg in 2026, a lower deficit figure than BMI's 400,000 t.

items.label: Transboundary water dispute escalation (0 calm / 1 tense / 2 crisis)
items.rationale: The IWT is formally suspended by India (treaty contested/abeyance = rubric level 1 threshold met) and official war threats have been exchanged at cabinet/army-chief level by both sides, satisfying the 'official threats exchanged' criterion. No confirmed deliberate flow cut-off physically executed nor an actual armed clash over water has been verified, so level 2 is not triggered.
items.fact: India's MEA reaffirmed on 2026-07-03 that the Indus Waters Treaty 'stands in abeyance' (IndiaTV News, July 3 2026); Pakistan's defence minister Khawaja Asif threatened war ('we will go to war') in late June 2026 (CNBC, June 22 2026), while India's water minister CR Patil stated 'not a single drop of water will go to Pakistan' (Dawn/ANI, ~June 2026); Pakistan reported abnormal Chenab flow variations three times (twice 2025, once May 2026) but no confirmed deliberate sustained physical cut-off has been verified — India currently lacks infrastructure for full diversion (CSIS, Al Jazeera).

dissent.view: Some analysts argue India's short-period dam manipulations at Baglihar/Kishanganga in 2025 and anomalous Chenab flows in May 2026 constitute early-stage weaponization of water flows, potentially approaching a partial flow cut-off (Chatham House, April 2026; Pakistan Indus Commissioner, Dawn June 30 2026)

GERD/Nile dispute also registers as TENSE: Egypt-Sudan issued official joint threats against Ethiopia's unilateral GERD operation (Feb 26 2026), Egypt's Sisi warned of 'necessary measures' (Oct 2025), and negotiations are at a declared 'dead end' — but no kinetic action or physical flow cut-off confirmed

items.label: SEC central clearing on track (0 ok / 1 slipping / 2 delayed)
items.rationale: A first formal extension was already granted (Feb 2025), which by itself maps to rubric level 1. Additionally, exemptive-relief requests were pending and one has now been conditionally granted (Jun 23, 2026), further confirming level 1. There is no evidence of a second deadline extension or a passed deadline, so level 2 is not warranted.
items.fact: The SEC granted a first one-year extension on Feb 25, 2025 (SEC Release 34-102487), shifting cash clearing to Dec 31, 2026 and repo to June 30, 2027. No second extension has been announced. However, the SEC published SIFMA's inter-affiliate exemptive-relief request on Apr 17, 2026 (comment deadline May 29, 2026), and on Jun 23, 2026 the Federal Register (2026-12613) shows the SEC issued a conditional exemptive order related to the inter-affiliate exclusion from the Trade Submission Requirement. The core compliance dates remain unchanged as of today. Source: sec.gov/newsroom/press-releases/2025-43; federalregister.gov/documents/2026/06/23/2026-12613; SEC Commissioner Uyeda statement Apr 20, 2026.

items.label: AI capex-vs-revenue gap concern (0 closing / 1 wide / 2 widening fast)
items.rationale: Capex growth (77-84%+ YoY) materially outpaces AI-attributable revenue growth (28-63% YoY), confirming the gap is WIDE (rubric 1). However, AI cloud revenues are still accelerating strongly on a YoY basis with no QoQ deceleration reported, and no hyperscaler raised capex while simultaneously cutting AI-revenue guidance, so rubric 2 is not triggered.
items.fact: Q1 2026 earnings (April 29, 2026): combined hyperscaler 2026 capex ~$725B, up 77% YoY per Financial Times; Microsoft Q3 FY26 capex $30.88B +84% YoY vs AI revenue run rate >$37B +123% YoY; Alphabet capex $35.67B >2x YoY vs Google Cloud +63% YoY to $20B; Amazon capex $44.2B vs AWS +28% YoY to $37.6B. Allianz Research (March 2026, allianz.com PDF) quantifies the capex-to-revenue growth divergence at ~46%, exceeding the 32% seen in the 2001 telecom excess cycle. AI revenue is still strongly accelerating YoY across all three cloud hyperscalers; no hyperscaler cut its AI-revenue outlook.

items.label: Govt stability (0 stable / 1 fragile / 2 collapse-or-snap-election)
items.rationale: France has a hung parliament since the 2024 snap election; Lecornu leads a minority government that has survived multiple confidence challenges by assembling ad hoc support, without a working majority — squarely Rubric 1 (FRAGILE). No dissolution has been called and the government has not fallen, ruling out Rubric 2.
items.fact: PM Sébastien Lecornu's minority government faces a new no-confidence motion filed by Green lawmakers on July 2, 2026 (Reuters, France24), scheduled for a National Assembly vote on July 6; the motion has only ~58 backers vs. 289 needed and is widely expected to fail, as RN has declined to support it. The government previously survived no-confidence votes in January and February 2026 and passed the 2026 budget under Article 49.3-linked pressure.

items.label: Pension-fund leverage event, UK-2022 type (0 none / 1 stress / 2 blowup)
items.rationale: Both the primary regulators (ESMA, June 2026; TPR, 2025) explicitly state buffers are adequate, leverage is lower, and operations are normal — matching Rubric 0 (adequate buffers, no margin stress). No regulator flags a live stress episode, no forced gilt selling and no central-bank intervention has occurred in 2026; the residual tail-risk note from ESMA on a subset of funds is a forward-looking caveat, not a current flag, so it does not elevate the reading to 1.
items.fact: ESMA TRV Risk Analysis (23 Jun 2026, ESMA50-1949966494-4208): LDI fund resilience has improved since 2022 mini-budget crisis; liquidity shortfall in stress tests fell from EUR 9bn to EUR 3bn (end-2024); LDI market NAV is EUR 113bn vs EUR 130bn at crisis peak; a subset of funds may still face challenges in a large interest rate shock but no current margin-call episode or forced selling is identified. TPR Market Oversight report (covering data to 31 Mar 2025) confirms LDI buffers raised to ~300 bps from ~150 bps pre-2022, leverage reduced, and 'LDI related operations have been normal' during 2025 rate volatility episodes.

items.label: USD share of FX reserves (%, IMF COFER)
items.rationale: The rubric requires the latest published USD share of allocated global FX reserves as a percent; the IMF COFER brief dated March 27, 2026 reports 56.77% for Q4 2025, which is the most recent official release. Note that from 2025Q3 onward IMF eliminated the allocated/unallocated distinction so this figure now covers 100% of world FX reserves, making it directly comparable to the prior 'allocated' series.
items.fact: IMF COFER Data Brief (March 27, 2026): 'The share of US dollar holdings decreased to 56.77 percent in 2025Q4, from 56.93 percent in 2025Q3.' Total FX reserves = $13.14 trillion. Source: https://data.imf.org/en/news/imf%20data%20brief%20march%2027

items.label: China PPI YoY (%) - large swing either way is worse
items.rationale: The rubric requires the signed YoY figure as published by NBS; +3.9% for May 2026 is that figure. Per the rubric, the engine scores on the SIZE of the move — +3.9% is the fastest pace since July 2022, a sharp cost-push inflation print that flags input-cost pressure spillover risk.
items.fact: NBS official press release (stats.gov.cn/english/PressRelease/202606/t20260611_1963929.html, released 2026-06-11): 'In May 2026, China's producer price index for industrial products (PPI) increased by 3.9% year on year and 0.5% month on month.' Corroborated by CNBC (2026-06-10) and TradingEconomics citing same NBS release.

items.label: New tariff/anti-dumping actions (0 calm / 1 rising / 2 escalating)
items.rationale: At least 5 distinct new measures against China were recorded in the trailing 3 months (March 12–July 3, 2026), AND the June 2 USTR forced-labor determination proposes a 12.5% tariff across all Chinese goods — satisfying the rubric threshold of >=4 new measures OR a broad tariff round (>=10% across a sector). Although the November 2025 Busan truce formally holds and China has not retaliated, the volume and breadth of new US measures maps unambiguously to ESCALATING (2).
items.fact: USTR on June 2, 2026 formally determined all 60 investigated economies (including China) actionable and proposed 12.5% Section 301 tariffs on Chinese goods across all non-excluded categories under forced-labor investigation (USTR.gov, 2026-06-02). Additionally, in Apr–Jul 2026 window: (1) March 11-12 USTR initiated Section 301 investigations on excess capacity and forced labor naming China; (2) June 1 Trump proclamation adjusted Section 232 duties on aluminum/copper/steel affecting China; (3) June 9 US added Alibaba/Baidu/BYD to Chinese Military Companies list; (4) Federal Register June 12-17 recorded multiple new preliminary AD/CVD determinations on Chinese van trailers, PV cells, fiberglass door panels. Proposed 12.5% forced-labor Section 301 tariff covers virtually all goods — a broad tariff round. China condemned but issued no counter-measures as of July 3.

items.label: Taiwan / cross-strait tension (0 calm / 1 elevated / 2 crisis)
items.rationale: The environment is elevated—PLA carrier transits, ongoing CCG maritime coercion east of Taiwan, and a background pattern of regularized blockade-rehearsal drills (most recently Dec 2025)—but sortie volumes are declining and no kinetic clash or actual blockade has been imposed, firmly placing this at Level 1. Level 2 is not warranted as there is no blockade, no kinetic engagement, and no full mobilization.
items.fact: ISW-AEI China & Taiwan Update (July 2, 2026): Taiwan's defense ministry reported 12 days in June 2026 without any ADIZ incursions across the median line; PLA monthly sorties have reverted to the pre-Lai baseline (from ~300+ to ~200/month) since early 2026; no high-altitude balloons into Taiwan's ADIZ since February 2026. The most recent named large-scale drill was 'Justice Mission 2025' (Dec 29-30, 2025), a multi-domain blockade rehearsal. In late June 2026, the PLA carrier Fujian transited the Taiwan Strait (~June 24, 2026) while Taiwan ran a five-day Immediate Combat Readiness Exercise (launched June 22). No kinetic clash or actual blockade has occurred.

items.label: US domestic RE producer blacklist (0 none / 1 entity-specific ban / 2 sector-wide ban)
items.rationale: Two named US rare-earth producers (MP Materials Corp. and USA Rare Earth, Inc.) are on the MOFCOM list as of 22 June 2026, satisfying rubric level 1 exactly. The action is explicitly entity-specific (10 named firms), not a categorical ban on the US rare-earth sector as a class, and multiple analysts and Benchmark Minerals confirm it is 'entity-specific rather than a blanket restriction on rare earth exports to the US,' precluding level 2.
items.fact: On 22 June 2026, China's MOFCOM issued Announcement No. 23 of 2026, adding 10 named US entities to its export control list under the Export Control Law and Regulations on Dual-Use Items. The two US rare-earth producers explicitly named are MP Materials Corp. and USA Rare Earth, Inc. The remaining eight entities are defense/drone firms (Aveox, Red Cat Holdings, Teal Drones, IMSAR, Jaia Robotics, Ball Aerospace & Technologies, Oshkosh Defense, L3Harris Maritime Services). Multiple corroborating sources: Al Jazeera (2026-06-22), Benchmark Minerals (2026-06-22), Bloomberg (2026-06-22), SMM/Metal.com (2026-06-23). Share-price reaction was muted—MP Materials roughly flat, USA Rare Earth modestly higher on announcement day—consistent with entity-specific rather than sector-wide disruption.

items.label: Food-price trend (0 falling / 1 elevated / 2 spiking)
items.rationale: The index is falling MoM (-0.3%) but still elevated YoY (+2.2%), failing the rubric's requirement that BOTH MoM and YoY be negative for a score of 0; MoM change is far below the 5% threshold and the 3-month gain (~+1.4%) is far below 10%, so SPIKING does not apply either.
items.fact: FAO Food Price Index averaged 130.3 points in June 2026, down 0.3% MoM from May 2026 (130.8) but 2.2% higher than a year earlier. Source: FAO via bizcommunity.com, July 3, 2026 (FAO June 2026 release).

flags.name: Redemption gates widespread, but HY spreads benign
flags.message: Large-fund redemption gates are rated WIDESPREAD (2) but high-yield spreads are benign. Broad gating usually coincides with visible credit stress; confirm the gates are systemic, not a couple of isolated funds.

indicators.label: Large-fund redemption gates (0 none / 1 isolated / 2 widespread)
US high-yield OAS (%, ≈310bps)

s1_basis_trade.label: Hidden leverage in US government bonds
s1_basis_trade.gist: Hedge funds borrow heavily to trade US government bonds; if that trade unwinds fast it can freeze the market everyone treats as safe.

s2_japan.label: Japan's rates and the global cash carry trade
s2_japan.gist: Cheap Japanese money has funded bets worldwide for years; as Japan's interest rates rise, pulling that money home can ripple through global markets.

s3_private_credit.label: Private lending you can't see into
s3_private_credit.gist: A fast-growing pile of loans sits outside the banks with little public disclosure, so stress there is hard to spot until it is already large.

s4_dev_debt.label: Debt stress in developing countries
s4_dev_debt.gist: Many poorer nations owe more than they can comfortably repay; a wave of defaults can spread and pull in their lenders.

s5_china_deflation.label: China's prices spilling into the world
s5_china_deflation.gist: Big swings in China's factory prices - falling or rising - get exported to everyone else as cheaper goods or higher input costs.

s6_rare_earths.label: Rare-earth minerals used as a weapon
s6_rare_earths.gist: China controls most of the world's rare-earth processing and is now restricting it, squeezing US defense and high-tech manufacturing.

s7_stablecoins.label: Crypto dollars and US government debt
s7_stablecoins.gist: Stablecoins must hold US Treasuries as backing, so their rapid growth - or a sudden wobble - now feeds straight back into the government-debt market.

s8_ai_capex.label: The AI spending boom
s8_ai_capex.gist: Enormous borrowing to build AI data centers is racing ahead of the revenue meant to justify it, concentrating the risk in a few giant companies.

s9_cre.label: Commercial real estate and small banks
s9_cre.gist: Half-empty offices and loans coming due strain the regional banks that hold them - a slow-burning credit risk.

s10_france.label: France and eurozone stability
s10_france.gist: A politically gridlocked, heavily indebted France is the soft spot that could reopen broader euro-area stress.

s11_consumer_debt.label: Household debt strain
s11_consumer_debt.gist: Rising late payments on car loans and buy-now-pay-later show households stretching - an early warning on consumer health.

s12_gold_dedollar.label: Gold and moving away from the dollar
s12_gold_dedollar.gist: Central banks stockpiling gold and building non-dollar payment systems slowly chip away at the dollar's dominance.

s13_insurance.label: Insurers pulling back
s13_insurance.gist: As disaster losses climb, insurers raise prices or quit whole regions, leaving homes and assets exposed.

s14_pensions.label: Pensions and an aging population
s14_pensions.gist: Aging populations and underfunded pensions strain public budgets; leveraged pension funds can also break in a sudden rate shock, as the UK saw in 2022.

s15_ai_power.label: AI's strain on the power grid
s15_ai_power.gist: AI data centers need more electricity than the grid can quickly supply, triggering curtailments, building moratoria, and multi-year connection queues.

s16_copper.label: Copper for electrification
s16_copper.gist: Electrifying everything needs far more copper than mines can deliver, and major mine outages are tightening supply further.

s17_water.label: Water as a hidden constraint
s17_water.gist: Droughts and shrinking reservoirs threaten chip factories, farms, and power generation - an underpriced input with no substitute.

s18_food_fertilizer.label: Food and fertilizer supply
s18_food_fertilizer.gist: Export curbs and shipping disruptions to fertilizer raise the risk of higher food prices down the line.

s19_lithography.label: The chipmaking chokepoint
s19_lithography.gist: The most advanced chips depend on one Dutch tool-maker and Taiwan's factories; any disruption there hits the entire tech economy.

s20_sea_lanes.label: Shipping lanes and undersea cables
s20_sea_lanes.gist: Key straits like Hormuz and the undersea cables carrying the internet are vulnerable chokepoints for global trade and data.

s21_clearinghouses.label: The financial system's plumbing
s21_clearinghouses.gist: Clearinghouses sit at the center of every market; if one seized up under stress, it would jam trading everywhere at once.

playbook.disclaimer: Educational cause-and-effect only - how stress like this has historically moved through the system. Nothing here is investment advice or a prediction.

s1_basis_trade.mechanism: A rates shock forces highly leveraged government-bond arbitrage positions to unwind at once: margin calls trigger forced Treasury selling, liquidity in the world's benchmark market evaporates, and everything priced off it reprices.
benefits: cash and short-term bills through the panic
long-term government bonds AFTER central-bank intervention
s1_basis_trade.watch: The funding-spread and bond-volatility rows above; the historical pivot was the central bank announcing a backstop facility.
s1_basis_trade.trap: In past episodes the 'safe' asset itself fell first - forced sellers sell what they can - so the flight-to-safety instinct lost money until the intervention.

s2_japan.mechanism: Surging Japanese yields pull decades of cheap yen funding home: the yen spikes on repatriation, and leveraged positions worldwide that were financed in yen get liquidated together - hitting high-momentum equities and emerging markets hardest.
benefits: Japanese banks and insurers as rates normalize
s2_japan.watch: The 30-year JGB row above (live official feed) and the yen; past unwinds announced themselves through the currency.
s2_japan.trap: Historical carry unwinds were violent but brief - the 2024 episode round-tripped within days; the durable shift was in Japanese financials, not the panic.

s3_private_credit.mechanism: One honest markdown forces everyone's marks: investor withdrawals hit gates, gated funds fire-sell into an illiquid market, and lending to mid-sized companies contracts just as they need to refinance.
bleeds: private-credit vehicles trading below stated asset value
benefits: holders of dry powder buying discounted credit afterwards
public high-quality credit as the flight destination
s3_private_credit.watch: The default-rate and redemption-gate rows above; public high-yield spreads are the honest proxy for hidden marks.
s3_private_credit.trap: Opacity means the repricing arrives as a sudden gap, not a trend - by the time it is public, the move has largely happened.

s4_dev_debt.mechanism: A cluster of sovereign defaults - not one - overwhelms the restructuring machinery: redemptions from emerging-market debt funds force indiscriminate selling of strong and weak credits alike, and stress spreads through common creditors.
benefits: and historically the buyers of defaulted debt after restructuring - among the best-documented recoveries in fixed income
s4_dev_debt.watch: The new-IMF-programs row above and the dollar's strength, which is usually the upstream cause.
s4_dev_debt.trap: Index diversification means one or two defaults barely register; the systemic wave this story warns of is exactly what official bailouts exist to prevent.

s5_china_deflation.mechanism: Deepening factory-gate deflation is exported as cheaper goods, compressing margins for competing producers everywhere - unless Beijing's stimulus response whipsaws the pressure into a sudden commodity and China-equity rebound.
benefits: long-term bonds in the deflation leg
Chinese equities and industrial metals in the stimulus leg
s5_china_deflation.watch: The producer-price row above and any stimulus announcement - the two legs point opposite directions.
s5_china_deflation.trap: Policy whipsaw: historically every stimulus rumor violently reversed the deflation trade within days.

s6_rare_earths.mechanism: Export restrictions tighten into denial: magnet and heavy-rare-earth supply to defense, vehicle, and robotics manufacturers halts, panic stockpiling begins, and processing capacity outside China re-rates from commodity business to strategic asset.
bleeds: vehicle and defense production schedules in the short run
s6_rare_earths.watch: The license-flow, blacklist, and ex-China premium rows above - the premium is the market's own severity gauge.
s6_rare_earths.trap: Historically these moves reversed as fast as they started on any truce headline, and diversified funds in the space often hold the Chinese producers too.

s7_stablecoins.mechanism: A run on a major stablecoin forces its reserves - tens of billions in short-term government debt - to liquidate within days, briefly dislocating the bill market while the crypto ecosystem's dollar rails seize and leveraged positions cascade.
benefits: historically the surviving issuer gained lasting market share after each depeg scare
s7_stablecoins.watch: The live peg-deviation and supply rows above (updated daily from the aggregator) - past runs announced themselves in the peg first.
s7_stablecoins.trap: Crypto markets have a long history of face-ripping rebounds inside downtrends; past episodes punished conviction in both directions.

s8_ai_capex.mechanism: The debt-financed buildout meets a revenue shortfall: one credit event at a leveraged data-center operator reprices all AI-adjacent borrowing, capital-spending guidance gets cut, chip orders fall, and power-demand forecasts deflate - the whole complex moves together because it was funded together.
bleeds: power producers priced on AI demand
benefits: and - the dot-com lesson - the post-bust survivors compounded for a decade
s8_ai_capex.watch: The capex-gap, revenue-gap, and refinancing rows above; credit stress historically led equity repricing.
